programs.name: Oregon Health Plan (OHP)
programs.tagline: Free comprehensive health coverage for Oregon seniors, including medical care, prescriptions, and long-term care services.

localEntities.name: Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC) centers statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and has limited income or needs long-term care, Oregon Health Plan provides free comprehensive health coverage with no premiums or copays. This includes all medical care, prescriptions, dental, and long-term care services like nursing home care, assisted living, or home-based support.

For seniors needing long-term care, income must be under $2,982/month for singles. Because OHP covers everything under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers or worry about Medicare gaps. The program also protects your parent's home and personal belongings while covering care costs.

OHP operates through regional Coordinated Care Organizations (CCOs) that manage all services in your area, creating a single point of contact for your parent's healthcare needs.

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services: Must meet Nursing Home Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar assistance)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at ONE.Oregon.gov or call 855-673-2372 for seniors — processing times vary.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, residency, and medical records if applying for long-term care services

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at ONE.Oregon.gov or call 855-ORE-ADRC (855-673-2372) for phone assistance specific to older adults

steps.title: Complete assessment if needed
steps.description: If your parent needs long-term care, a clinical assessment will determine if they meet Nursing Home Level of Care requirements

steps.title: Get CCO assignment
steps.description: Once approved, your parent will be assigned to a regional Coordinated Care Organization that manages all their healthcare services

applicationGuide.tip: For seniors needing long-term care, call the ADRC line (855-673-2372) rather than the general number — they specialize in older adult applications.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits for Long-Term Care (OSIPM)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits for Oregon Supplemental Income Program-Medical (OSIPM). Different limits apply for general OHP Plus coverage.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Life insurance with face value up to $1,500

tiers.name: OHP Plus (General)
tiers.description: For adults 19-64 or those not needing long-term care

tiers.name: OSIPM (Long-term Care)
tiers.description: For seniors 65+ needing nursing home level care
tiers.coverage: All medical services plus nursing home, assisted living, adult foster care, or home-based support

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of all assets and resources

categories.name: Medical (if applying for long-term care)
items: Medical records showing need for daily assistance

contentSections.text: Two different programs with different rules: General OHP Plus has no asset limits but lower income limits. OSIPM for long-term care has higher income limits but strict $2,000 asset limits. Make sure you're applying for the right one.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for OHP long-term care?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit as long as they intend to return home or if a spouse or disabled child lives there.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $2,982 limit?
faqs.answer: He may still qualify through a spend-down process, where medical expenses reduce his countable income to below the limit. The program also has spousal protections if he's married.

faqs.question: Does OHP work with Medicare?
faqs.answer: Yes, OHP can work as secondary coverage to Medicare for dual-eligible seniors, though drug coverage may be limited. OHP will cover costs that Medicare doesn't.

faqs.question: How much money can Mom keep if she's in a nursing home?
faqs.answer: Nursing home residents keep about $77/month for personal needs ($90/month if they receive VA pension). OHP pays the facility directly for care costs.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the CCOs in different areas?
faqs.answer: All CCOs must provide the same core benefits, but they may have different provider networks and some additional services. You'll be assigned to the CCO that serves your parent's address.

faqs.question: Can undocumented immigrants get OHP?
faqs.answer: As of July 2023, Oregon expanded OHP to cover people regardless of immigration status, though specific benefits may vary. Call 855-673-2372 to discuss your parent's situation.

programs.name: OPI-M (Medicaid-funded)
programs.tagline: Free in-home care services and caregiver support for Oregon adults who need daily living assistance

localEntities.name: Local Aging and People with Disabilities (APD) offices via https://www.oregon.gov/odhs/pages/office-finder.aspx

programs.intro:
If your parent needs help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or getting around, OPI-M provides free in-home personal care services through Oregon's Medicaid program. Because OPI-M has higher income limits than traditional Medicaid (up to $5,217/month for a single person in 2025), many families who don't qualify for other programs can get help here.

The program covers personal care services, home safety modifications, meal delivery, emergency response systems, and support services for you as the family caregiver. Your parent must live in their own home or with family — not in assisted living or residential care facilities. Once approved, coverage continues for 24 months even if income or assets change.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need assistance with Activities of Daily Living (transferring, mobility, eating, toileting, bathing, dressing) and meet Service Priority Levels 1-18
otherRequirements: Lives in own home or with family (not facilities)
Cannot receive other Medicaid long-term services simultaneously

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your local APD office in-person or by mail; requires in-home assessment.

steps.title: Find your local APD office
steps.description: Use the office finder at oregon.gov/odhs/pages/office-finder.aspx to locate your area office

steps.title: Download and complete application
steps.description: Get the OPI-M application form and gather required documents (income, residency, medical records)

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Deliver completed application to your local APD office in-person or by mail

steps.title: Schedule home assessment
steps.description: A Case Manager will contact you to schedule an in-home functional assessment

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies; requires in-home assessment scheduling
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is already receiving other Medicaid long-term services, you'll need to choose between programs — you cannot receive OPI-M simultaneously with other services.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits
contentSections.footnote: 2025 limits at 400% Federal Poverty Level. For couples, only applicant's income counts — spouse's income is disregarded.

contentSections.text: Major advantage: Once approved, you keep coverage for 24 consecutive months even if income or assets increase. No Medicaid Estate Recovery applies to this program.

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Oregon residency (utility bills, lease agreement)

categories.name: Medical
items: Doctor's assessment of daily living needs

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent must live in their own home or with family to qualify for OPI-M — the program specifically excludes people in assisted living or residential care facilities.

faqs.question: What if my parent is already getting other Medicaid services?
faqs.answer: You cannot receive OPI-M simultaneously with other Medicaid long-term services like SPPC or APD Waiver programs. You would need to choose between OPI-M and their current services.

faqs.question: Will the state try to recover costs from my parent's estate?
faqs.answer: No. OPI-M is specifically exempt from Medicaid Estate Recovery, so the state will not seek repayment from your parent's estate after they pass away.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income increases after approval?
faqs.answer: Nothing for 24 months. OPI-M provides continuous coverage for two full years even if income or assets change during that time.

faqs.question: Can I get support as the family caregiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you provide 10+ hours per week of in-person care for 3+ months. The program includes flexible caregiver support services, and you don't have to be a relative to qualify.

faqs.question: My parent is 45 and disabled — can they qualify?
faqs.answer: Potentially, but they must meet OSIPM disability criteria and cannot have intellectual/developmental disabilities or mental health/substance use disorders (unless specific exceptions are met).

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing-home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE covers all medical care and daily assistance with no co-pays or deductibles.

localEntities.name: Providence PACE (serves Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, Clatsop counties)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, PACE may provide an alternative to nursing home placement. Because PACE covers all medical care, prescriptions, adult day programs, and in-home services under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers — one interdisciplinary team manages everything.

PACE has no income or asset limits for enrollment itself. However, if your parent needs Medicaid to pay for it, they must have income under $2,901/month and assets of $2,000 or less (excluding their home). Private pay is also available, though specific monthly costs aren't publicly listed.

The major limitation: PACE operates only in specific service areas within Oregon. Your parent must live in or be willing to relocate to a PACE service area, and they must agree to receive all healthcare exclusively through the PACE program.

summary: Can live safely at home with support
Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as needing Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs the kind of daily care and supervision normally provided in a nursing home)
otherRequirements: Must live in a PACE program service area
Must be able to reside safely in the community with PACE support
Must agree to receive all health and long-term care services exclusively from PACE providers

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging for assessment — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: They will assess whether your parent meets the Nursing Facility Level of Care requirement using Oregon's Service Priority Level 1-13 assessment.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: The state will evaluate whether your parent needs nursing-home-level care and can live safely in the community with PACE support.

steps.title: Verify service area eligibility
steps.description: Confirm your parent lives in or can relocate to an approved PACE service area.

steps.title: Choose payment method
steps.description: Determine if your parent qualifies for Medicaid coverage or will pay private fees directly to PACE.

applicationGuide.tip: The only reason for denial is if your parent cannot live safely in the community even with PACE support — this decision can be appealed.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (Medicaid-funded participants only)
excluded: Burial plots and funeral funds up to $1,500

offices.name: Providence PACE
offices.address: Serves Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, and Clatsop counties

contentSections.text: PACE requires your parent to use only PACE providers for all healthcare. They cannot see outside doctors or specialists without PACE approval — this is a binding commitment.
PACE is not tied to Oregon's Medicaid waiver waitlists. If your parent is on a waiting list for other home care programs, PACE may provide an alternative route to services.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep seeing their current doctor if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE requires participants to receive all healthcare exclusively from PACE providers and their contracted specialists. Your parent would need to switch to PACE's medical team.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid but they can't afford private pay?
faqs.answer: PACE accepts private pay participants, but specific monthly costs aren't publicly available. Contact Providence PACE directly for pricing. There are no income limits for PACE enrollment itself — only for Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: Can my parent live in assisted living and still use PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as they can live safely in the community with PACE support. PACE serves people in their own homes, assisted living facilities, and adult foster care homes.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent needs emergency care outside the PACE service area?
faqs.answer: PACE covers emergency care anywhere, but routine care must be coordinated through PACE providers. For ongoing care outside the service area, your parent would need to disenroll from PACE.

faqs.question: How is the nursing facility level of care assessment done?
faqs.answer: Oregon uses a Service Priority Level 1-13 assessment conducted by the local Area Agency on Aging. This evaluates your parent's need for help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, and mobility.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for Medicaid through PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is exempt from Medicaid's $2,000 asset limit, regardless of its value. Your parent can keep their house and still qualify for Medicaid coverage of PACE services.
